demography.note: Working-age pool roughly flat to 2050 (UN WPP 2024 medium variant) — immigration is the swing variable, not fertility.

energy.note: Domestic shale + LNG give real supply slack, but the AI buildout has moved the actual constraint onto the grid — interconnection queues (constraint.grid-interconnection-us) and turbine/transformer lead times, not fuel.

chips.note: Owns design (NVIDIA, AMD) and EDA/IP chokepoints, and is buying back fab capacity (TSMC Arizona, Micron), but still has zero domestic leading-edge logic fab in volume as of this vintage.

fiscal.note: Reserve-currency privilege funds a large twin deficit that would sink a normal sovereign — durable while the dollar's role holds, not indefinitely.

stability.note: High institutional continuity, but policy whiplash on trade/export-control stance across administrations is itself a source of volatility for counterparties.

gov_focus.basis: Hyperscaler capex flood (tree.capex) plus explicit federal posture treating compute as strategic — the binding constraint the government is actually chasing is power, not chips.
Oct 2022 + subsequent rounds of controls treat advanced compute as a controlled export (tree.export); enforcement scope has widened, not narrowed, each cycle.
CHIPS Act-funded fab reshoring (TSMC AZ, Micron, Samsung Taylor) is real capital but on a 4-5yr fab lead time — visible in spend now, not yet in output.
Federal and state permitting pushes for gas turbines and nuclear restarts are a direct response to datacenter power demand outpacing grid buildout.

flows.basis: CHIPS Act + hyperscaler capex co-funding TSMC's overseas fab expansion program.
US hyperscaler and chipmaker deals (e.g. Microsoft–G42) route US capital and compute access into Gulf AI buildouts under revised export terms.
Long-running H-1B/skilled-immigration pipeline in engineering and AI research; the largest single bilateral tech-talent flow in this set.
HBM and DRAM imports feed directly into the US AI accelerator supply chain (NVIDIA/AMD packaging).

demography.note: Working-age population already past its peak (widely dated to the mid-2010s) and contracting through 2050 — the fastest large-economy demographic reversal in this set.

energy.note: Builds grid and generation capacity (coal, nuclear, renewables) faster than any other economy here; power is a state-directed lever, not a binding constraint the way it is for the US.

chips.note: Cut off from leading-edge lithography (no EUV access) and SMIC is stuck well behind the frontier on logic, but the DeepSeek shock (tree.openw) showed real algorithmic workaround capacity under the constraint, and legacy-node capacity is expanding fast.

fiscal.note: High state capacity to direct spending, offset by a large local-government and property-sector debt overhang that predates and outlasts any single stimulus cycle.

stability.note: Low day-to-day political volatility under centralized control, but capital controls, opaque local debt, and the demographic cliff are slow-moving stability risks, not fast ones.

gov_focus.basis: State-directed capital (Big Fund rounds, SMIC/Huawei investment) treats mature and eventually leading-edge chip production as a national-security priority, not just an industrial one.
Beijing's export-control counterstrike on rare earths and processing (atlas.beijingec) explicitly mirrors and retaliates against US chip controls — a live, escalating lever.
Same counterstrike posture applied more broadly — gallium, germanium, and graphite export licensing used as negotiating leverage.
Retirement-age increases and pro-natalist subsidies are underway but modest relative to the scale of the projected contraction — an acknowledged, not yet solved, problem.

flows.basis: Discounted Russian crude and pipeline gas redirected to China after Western sanctions cut off the European market.
Still one of the largest holders of US Treasuries despite a multi-year diversification trend away from dollar reserves.
China remains a large end-market for South Korean memory even as leading-edge logic access is cut off.

demography.note: One of the steepest working-age declines in this set, compounding the concentration risk already sitting on top of TSMC.

energy.note: Import-dependent (LNG + coal), a small and exposed grid, and a live nuclear-phaseout debate — genuinely one of the more fragile energy positions of any economy this globally load-bearing.

chips.note: TSMC holds the overwhelming majority of leading-edge logic fabrication worldwide (atlas.tsmc) — the single highest-leverage chokepoint on this entire atlas, full stop.

fiscal.note: Sustained current-account surplus driven almost entirely by semiconductor exports; fiscally strong but that strength is itself a single-industry bet.

stability.note: Existential geopolitical risk (PRC reunification pressure, atlas.taiwanstrait) dominates every other stability input — this is the one score in the set where the economics are strong and the tail risk is the whole story.

gov_focus.basis: TSMC's Arizona, Japan (Kumamoto), and Germany (Dresden) fabs are a deliberate 'silicon shield' diversification play, explicitly discussed in Taiwanese government and TSMC investor communications.
LNG terminal expansion and renewables buildout are direct responses to how exposed fab-scale power demand is to any supply disruption.
Already dominant at the leading edge; policy focus here is defensive retention of the lead (R&D subsidy, talent retention) rather than catching up.

flows.basis: The overwhelming majority of leading-edge AI accelerator and CPU die volume for US fabless designers is fabricated at TSMC.
TSMC's Kumamoto fab is a direct capital and technology transfer into Japan's chip-materials-adjacent ecosystem.

demography.note: The steepest working-age decline of any economy in this set, consistent with having the world's lowest total fertility rate for over a decade.

energy.note: Import-dependent with a domestically nuclear-heavy grid; adequate for current load but a real constraint on further heavy-industry or datacenter expansion without new nuclear or LNG capacity.

chips.note: SK Hynix and Samsung together hold the dominant share of HBM and DRAM production (constraint.hbm-qualification) — the memory half of the AI-accelerator supply chain runs through this country.

fiscal.note: Sustained export-driven current-account surplus; fiscally conservative by developed-economy standards.

stability.note: High institutional stability with a persistent but low-probability North Korea tail risk; the demographic collapse is a slower, larger, more certain risk than the security one.

gov_focus.basis: K-Chips Act-style subsidies and tax credits aim to keep HBM/memory fab investment domestic rather than losing it to US-based expansion incentives.
Government has run a large, explicitly-acknowledged pro-natalist subsidy program for years with limited measured effect on the fertility rate to date.

flows.basis: HBM/DRAM exports feed directly into US AI accelerator packaging (NVIDIA, AMD).
China remains a large memory export market despite the broader chip-control regime, since memory sits outside most leading-edge logic restrictions.

demography.note: Working-age population peaked three decades ago — the longest-running contraction of any economy in this set, and the one every other aging economy here is implicitly compared against.

energy.note: Post-Fukushima nuclear restart has been slow and politically contested; heavy LNG dependence keeps industrial power costs structurally high relative to the US or China.

chips.note: Doesn't compete at leading-edge logic volume, but the Tokyo materials/equipment complex (atlas.tokyomat) is a real chokepoint feeding every fab globally, and Rapidus is a live, high-risk bet to re-enter 2nm logic.

fiscal.note: Debt/GDP is the highest of any economy in this set (~260% by most recent published figures), offset historically by low borrowing costs from overwhelmingly domestic bond ownership — a structure under real pressure as the BOJ normalizes policy.

stability.note: Very high institutional and social stability; the demographic and fiscal pressures are slow-burning, not acute.

gov_focus.basis: Rapidus's government-backed 2nm logic bet is an explicit attempt to re-enter leading-edge fabrication after decades of ceding ground to TSMC and Samsung.
Automation and robotics deployment is being pursued as the primary policy substitute for the labor force Japan is not going to get back — an explicit, decades-old strategic choice.

flows.basis: TSMC's Kumamoto fab investment is a direct inbound capital and technology flow tied to Japan's materials-supply strength.
Semiconductor materials and equipment exports (photoresist, specialty chemicals) feed US fab capacity expansion.

demography.note: One of only three economies in this set with a growing working-age population through 2050 — the demographic dividend is real, but it only pays off if jobs and capital show up to meet it.

energy.note: Still coal-heavy with a large and fast renewables buildout underway, but grid reliability and industrial power availability remain a real constraint on manufacturing growth outside a few states.

chips.note: No domestic leading-edge or even mature-node fab in volume production as of this vintage — India Semiconductor Mission incentives are real but the country is still pre-fab, not post-fab, in this stack. Score reflects that honestly rather than crediting announced-but-unbuilt capacity.

fiscal.note: Fiscal deficit is manageable relative to GDP but large in absolute terms given population scale; improving trend over the past decade.

stability.note: Robust electoral democracy with real institutional continuity, offset by execution risk on large infrastructure and industrial-policy programs.

gov_focus.basis: India Semiconductor Mission subsidies have attracted announced fab and packaging investment (Micron, Tata), but as of this vintage none is yet at volume leading-edge production — this is an early-stage, not mature, policy push.

Explicit, high-stakes government framing: the working-age expansion is the central economic opportunity of the next 25 years, and job-creation policy is treated as existential to capturing it rather than routine.
Early-stage critical-mineral partnership agreements (with Australia, others) aimed at reducing dependence on China-controlled processing, still nascent.

flows.basis: The largest single source of skilled-immigration tech and engineering talent into the US by a wide margin, sustained over decades.

demography.note: Working-age population contracting despite very high immigration inflows relative to citizen population, a structural feature of the city-state model.

energy.note: Fully import-dependent with almost no domestic generation land or resources; grid capacity is the direct, explicit constraint behind the country's own past datacenter-construction moratorium — one of the tightest energy positions in this set.

chips.note: Not a leading-edge fab site, but a genuinely important assembly, test, and packaging hub (adjacent to atlas.ase-scale operations regionally) plus a growing wafer-fab presence — a mid-tier but real node in the stack, not a bystander.

fiscal.note: Among the strongest sovereign balance sheets in the world via GIC and Temasek's reserve holdings — this is the standout fiscal score in the set.

stability.note: Extremely high institutional and political stability; the real vulnerability is scale and regional-shock exposure, not internal governance.

gov_focus.basis: The Singapore–Johor data-center and compute corridor (atlas.sgjohor) is a direct policy response to domestic energy limits — capacity is being built just across the border rather than at home.
GIC and Temasek deploy sovereign capital into global tech and AI infrastructure at a scale far larger than the domestic economy, a long-running and explicit state strategy.
Regional power-grid interconnection (Laos-Thailand-Malaysia-Singapore) and LNG import diversification are direct responses to having essentially zero domestic generation slack.

flows.basis: GIC/Temasek are large, active investors in US tech and AI-infrastructure assets.
Both sovereign-wealth-heavy states co-invest in overlapping global AI and infrastructure deals.

demography.note: Positive working-age trend, but read this with real caution: the population is overwhelmingly expatriate-driven rather than citizen-fertility-driven, which is a fundamentally different mechanism than India's dividend and carries its own dependency risk.

energy.note: Oil wealth plus an aggressive nuclear (Barakah) and solar diversification push gives real slack — increasingly positioned as a power exporter into its own AI-compute buildout, not just an oil exporter.

chips.note: No domestic fabrication, but has become one of the largest single buyers/hosts of advanced AI compute globally (G42, Stargate UAE at atlas.uae-ai-cluster) under negotiated export terms — a demand-side, not supply-side, chip position.

fiscal.note: Very strong sovereign balance sheet via ADIA and Mubadala; oil-revenue-funded but increasingly diversified into tech and AI holdings.

stability.note: High short-run political stability under a consolidated monarchy; longer-run risk is concentration of power and dependence on continued Western compute-access terms.

gov_focus.basis: The Stargate UAE / Abu Dhabi AI Campus buildout (atlas.uae-ai-cluster) and G42's partnerships are an explicit, state-level bet to become a top-tier global AI compute hub.
Mubadala and ADIA route oil-derived capital into global tech and AI infrastructure at a scale well beyond domestic absorption capacity.
Barakah nuclear plant and solar buildout are explicitly framed around powering the AI-compute ambition, not just domestic consumption.

flows.basis: Mubadala/ADIA and G42 have made large, publicly disclosed investments and partnership deals with US AI and chip firms.
Overlapping Gulf sovereign-wealth co-investment in regional AI and infrastructure projects.

demography.note: The strongest working-age growth trend in this set — a genuine, citizen-driven youth bulge rather than an expatriate-inflow effect, which is the key structural difference from the UAE's demographic profile.

energy.note: Ghawar and Abqaiq (atlas.ghawar) remain the largest and lowest-cost conventional oil production complex on earth — the single strongest energy-control score in this set by a wide margin.

chips.note: No meaningful domestic fabrication capacity; PIF-funded AI ambitions are compute-demand plays (buying/hosting), not supply-chain positions.

fiscal.note: Vision 2030 diversification spending is enormous relative to non-oil revenue, and the budget's break-even oil price has risen with it — real strength funded by a commodity whose price the country doesn't fully control.

stability.note: High short-term political stability under consolidated leadership; the multi-decade Vision 2030 diversification bet carries real execution risk that won't resolve for years.

gov_focus.basis: PIF-funded AI and data-center investment (including stakes tied to global compute buildouts) mirrors the UAE playbook, at a slightly earlier stage of execution.
PIF is one of the largest sovereign wealth funds globally and has been an active, publicly disclosed investor in tech, gaming, and AI-adjacent assets.
Vision 2030's job-localization (Saudization) programs are a direct, explicit policy response to needing to absorb the youth bulge into non-oil employment.

flows.basis: PIF has made large, publicly disclosed investments across US tech, gaming, and infrastructure sectors.
China is one of Saudi Arabia's largest single crude-oil export markets.

demography.note: Mild working-age decline, among the gentlest in this set, cushioned by continued EU labor mobility.

energy.note: Domestic North Sea gas production is in structural decline; import-dependent with an aggressive but not yet complete renewables buildout on a small, dense grid.

chips.note: ASML (atlas.asml) is the sole global source of EUV lithography systems — arguably the single most concentrated, highest-leverage chokepoint anywhere in this atlas, ahead even of TSMC's fabrication share.

fiscal.note: AAA-rated, sustained current-account surplus, low debt/GDP — one of the strongest fiscal positions in this set.

stability.note: Very high institutional stability; the live tension is being caught between US export-control demands and Chinese market access for ASML, a genuinely difficult position for a country this economically dependent on one firm.

gov_focus.basis: The Dutch government has repeatedly tightened ASML export licensing to China under sustained US pressure — an ongoing, escalating policy tension explicitly tied to tree.export.
Already holds the dominant position in EUV; policy focus is retention and R&D continuity (with Zeiss/Germany) rather than catching up to anyone.

flows.basis: TSMC's entire leading-edge roadmap depends on ASML's EUV systems — the most concentrated single-supplier flow in this dataset.
Samsung and SK Hynix are also EUV customers, though smaller volume purchasers than TSMC.

demography.note: Working-age population in sustained decline, cushioned somewhat by EU and non-EU immigration relative to the natural-fertility trend alone.

energy.note: Post-nuclear-phaseout and post-Russian-pipeline-gas-shock, industrial energy costs have risen structurally; renewables buildout is real but storage and grid capacity lag demand — one of the more energy-stressed wealthy economies in this set.

chips.note: Zeiss's optics (atlas.zeiss) are a direct, load-bearing input into ASML's EUV systems — a narrow but genuinely critical chokepoint, not a broad fabrication base.

fiscal.note: Historically fiscally conservative (constitutional debt brake) but under real pressure from industrial-decline-driven revenue softness and rising defense/energy-transition spending demands.

stability.note: High institutional stability; the live slow-variable story is industrial-model stress in autos and chemicals from Chinese competition and energy costs, not political instability.

gov_focus.basis: LNG terminal buildout and renewables acceleration are a direct, urgent response to losing pipeline gas access after 2022 — the most acute energy-policy shift in this set.
Industrial policy responses to Chinese EV and chip competition (tariffs, subsidy proposals) reflect a broader de-risking posture, still contested domestically.

flows.basis: Zeiss optics are a direct input into ASML's EUV lithography systems, making Germany a co-dependent node in the same chokepoint.
US LNG exports have become a meaningful replacement for lost Russian pipeline gas volume.

demography.note: Working-age decline compounded by wartime casualties and a sustained emigration wave since 2022 — the composition of the loss (skilled emigration specifically) likely matters more than the headline number.

energy.note: Reserves and production capacity remain large, but Western market access is severed; the real story is redirection (to China/India at a discount) rather than reduced output.

chips.note: Almost entirely cut off from leading-edge and most mature-node chip access under export controls; forced onto parallel-import and grey-market channels for both consumer and military-adjacent electronics. Score reflects near-total exclusion, not a data gap.

fiscal.note: War-economy spending plus sanctions strain the budget, partially offset by redirected energy revenue; fiscal opacity means real confidence in any precise figure here is low — treat this score as directional, not measured.

stability.note: High uncertainty from sustained war-economy footing, sanctions pressure, and opaque elite dynamics — the lowest stability score in this set.

gov_focus.basis: On the receiving end of controls rather than imposing them — policy focus is sanctions evasion and parallel-import workaround infrastructure, an inversion of every other country's posture on this vector.
Active redirection of oil and gas export infrastructure toward Asian buyers (China, India) at a discount, replacing severed European pipeline and tanker routes.

flows.basis: Discounted crude and pipeline gas redirected to China after the loss of the European market.
India has become one of the largest buyers of discounted Russian seaborne crude since 2022.
